description:
# Tiny surgery; unblocks per-spec dead-arm elimination

## The smell

`ir_typer::narrow_for_cond` (~line 1390) refines envs across `If` branches
for `BinOp::And/Or/Eq/Neq` and `Prim::ListIsNil` — but NOT for
`Prim::TypeTest`. After `If(TypeTest(v, D), then, else)`, v's descr in
then_env stays unchanged, so the existing `ir_fold` TypeTest folder never
sees the singleton it would need to fold a redundant downstream test.

## What changes

Add a `Prim::TypeTest(v, descr)` arm to `narrow_for_cond`:

```rust
Prim::TypeTest(v, descr) => {
    let current = env.get(v).cloned().unwrap_or_else(Descr::any);
    then_env.insert(*v, current.intersect(descr));
    else_env.insert(*v, current.diff(descr));
}
```

## What stays

Everything else in narrow_for_cond. The And/Or/Eq/Neq/ListIsNil arms
already establish the pattern this follows.

## What's removed

Nothing. Pure addition; correctness-only.

## Acceptance

- New unit test in ir_typer: build an FnIr with TypeTest+If; assert
  then_env narrows v to D, else_env narrows v to current\D.
- All existing fixtures' goldens unchanged (no fold pass currently
  consumes the narrowing for multi-spec fns; single-spec fns may
  already benefit, monitor for byte-identical or strictly smaller).
- All three execution paths still pass.

## Risk

`current.diff(&D)` can produce `Descr::none()` when D fully covers
current. Verify no downstream pass crashes on a `none()` env entry
(none() is the empty type and already exists, but worth tests).

## Why it's first

Lowest LOC, lowest risk, no public API change. Strictly improves
information available to all downstream passes. Compounds with .43.B.

comments.text:
BLOCKED — surprise surfaced on first impl.

CHANGE: ~10 LOC adding Prim::TypeTest arm to narrow_for_cond as specified.

RESULT on ast_eval:
- expected.specs: 38793 → 25136 bytes (-35%)  ← spec set shrank
- expected.clif:  76276 → 81845 bytes (+7%)   ← CLIF grew
- Specific concrete loss: 'v40 = load.i64 ...;; v40 :: :add' became 'v40 :: any'

ROOT CAUSE (hypothesis, not verified): narrow_for_cond's output flows into recursive-call argument descrs at call sites. Those descrs are spec keys. Narrowing changes the keys → different spec set. In this fixture, some specs merged (good — fewer copies) but the merged spec has broader types than either input spec previously had (because the per-callsite specialization that USED to capture the narrowed view is gone — replaced by a single spec that handles both).

So narrowing made the typer's BRANCH-LOCAL info more precise but destabilized the spec set, and without per-spec fold (.43.B) consuming the new narrower envs, the net per-spec CLIF is broader and bigger.

ACCEPTANCE VIOLATION: ticket said 'byte-identical OR strictly smaller'. Actual: bigger CLIF. Reverted.

OPTIONS TO CONSIDER (need user direction):

(a) Bundle .43.A + .43.B as one atomic ticket. They're inseparable — A alone regresses, A+B should net-improve.

(b) Narrow more conservatively: only refine vars not used as call args (preserves spec-key stability). Possible but adds complexity and may give up most of the benefit.

(c) Investigate further: are we really losing info, or just changing specialization granularity? Maybe spec set shrinking is a real net win (less code in ICache) even if CLIF size grew. Need runtime benchmark, not just byte count.

(d) Investigate the underlying spec-key sensitivity. Why does narrowing in then-branch destabilize spec creation? Is there a stability invariant we should be preserving?

RECOMMENDATION: option (a). The arc as designed has A and B compounding; the surprise is that they don't compose additively in isolation. Bundle them and re-bless under combined acceptance.

But this needs user authorization — restructuring atomic units mid-arc per CLAUDE.md no-silent-deferrals.

title: fz-ul4.43.A: narrow_for_cond handles Prim::TypeTest
